name: Indian Canyon
short_description: BLM rockhounding site in southeastern Utah offering agate and jasper specimens in scenic canyon terrain. Free public access with no permit required.
description:
Indian Canyon is a productive rockhounding destination on public BLM land in San Juan County, Utah, near the town of Blanding. The canyon features excellent agate and jasper deposits embedded in wash gravels and exposed along canyon walls, making it accessible for collectors of all skill levels. The site benefits from the regional geology of the Colorado Plateau, where ancient Permian and Cretaceous sedimentary formations have been weathered and eroded to expose colorful silicate minerals.

The collecting here is straightforward - walk the canyon wash and examine the gravels, particularly after recent rains when material is exposed and easier to spot. Agate nodules ranging from marble-size to fist-sized specimens are common, along with attractive jasper pieces in reds, yellows, and browns. The canyon walls also show exposed mineral deposits that may weather loose material suitable for collection.

As BLM land, Indian Canyon requires no permit and allows personal use collection up to 25 pounds per day. This makes it an ideal destination for weekend rockhounders seeking quality specimens without the cost and restrictions of fee dig sites. The relatively easy access and moderate difficulty terrain make it suitable for families and beginners willing to hike into the canyon.

The scenic qualities of Indian Canyon add to the experience - the canyon walls display colorful geology and the area offers views of the surrounding high desert landscape. Morning light illuminates the canyon walls beautifully and makes spotting translucent agate easier. Bring plenty of water, as the site is somewhat remote and summer temperatures can be intense.
history: Indian Canyon sits within the Colorado Plateau province, where Permian and Cretaceous sedimentary layers have been uplifted and dissected by erosion over millions of years. The silica-rich formations in this region, particularly chalcedony-bearing horizons, have weathered and concentrated agate and jasper deposits in canyon gravels and exposed outcrops. The area has been known to local rockhounders for decades as a reliable source for quality specimens.
tips: Focus your search in the wash bottom where gravity concentrates heavier mineral material - agates and jasper accumulate in gravel bars and behind small obstacles. Look for the distinctive waxy luster and translucent qualities of chalcedony-family stones; agates often show subtle color banding when held to light. Use a small crack hammer or rock chisel to carefully extract nodules from softer surrounding matrix, and always check for hollow centers by listening for a hollow ring when tapped. After rain events, the wash often exposes fresh material, so visit within a few days of precipitation for best results.
terrain_notes: Indian Canyon features slickrock and sandy wash floors with moderate elevation around 6,000-6,500 feet. Vegetation is sparse high desert scrub typical of the Colorado Plateau. Walking into the canyon from the parking area is approximately 0.5 to 1.5 miles depending on where you choose to collect. Footing is moderately easy on the wash floor but rocky in places; canyon walls are relatively stable slickrock. Some scrambling over small obstacles may be necessary.
written_directions: From Blanding, take US 191 north approximately 12 miles. Look for BLM access signs or informal pullouts on the east side of the highway near mile marker 240. Park at the designated pullout and hike east into Indian Canyon wash. A high-clearance vehicle is helpful but not required for highway parking. Local knowledge or GPS coordinates are recommended as trailhead signage may be minimal.
road_conditions: US 191 is paved highway; parking area is unpaved pullout with possible washboard conditions
parking_notes: Informal BLM pullout with space for 3-5 vehicles. No facilities. Park off pavement to avoid blocking traffic.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Collect loose material and carefully extracted specimens only - do not fracture canyon walls or use power tools. Pack out all waste.
rules:
Personal use collection only
Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection permitted
No motorized excavation or heavy machinery
Collect loose material and carefully extracted specimens
Do not fracture canyon walls or deface formations
No power tools allowed
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Blanding, approximately 12 miles southwest. Gas, food, and supplies available.
accessibility_notes: Uneven rocky terrain with some slickrock and sandy wash. Hiking required to reach productive areas. Sturdy footwear essential. Not wheelchair accessible. Moderate physical fitness helpful for 1-2 mile walking distances. Terrain is exposed with minimal shade; summer heat can be intense.

faq.question: What types of agates can I find at Indian Canyon near Blanding, Utah?
faq.answer: Indian Canyon produces primarily banded agates and chalcedony specimens in browns, reds, and cream colors. Many pieces show distinctive banding patterns visible when held to light. Specimens typically range from marble-size to fist-sized, with occasional larger nodules. Look in the wash gravels for the waxy luster characteristic of quality agate material.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Indian Canyon free and do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, Indian Canyon is completely free BLM land requiring no permit. You can collect up to 25 pounds per day for personal use at no cost. This makes it an excellent budget-friendly alternative to commercial fee dig sites while still offering quality agate and jasper specimens.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Indian Canyon in Utah?
faq.answer: The ideal seasons are April through May and September through October when temperatures are moderate and the high desert landscape is most pleasant. Avoid mid-summer (June-August) when daytime temperatures frequently exceed 90-95 degrees Fahrenheit. Winter can bring occasional snow at this elevation, making access more difficult.

faq.question: Can I bring my family and kids to Indian Canyon for rockhounding?
faq.answer: Absolutely. Indian Canyon is family and kid-friendly for ages 8 and up. The canyon wash provides an easy walking surface suitable for children, and the hunt for agates and jasper makes for engaging exploration. Bring plenty of water and sun protection, as the area offers minimal shade. Supervision is recommended, especially around canyon walls.

faq.question: How do I find Indian Canyon rockhounding site from Blanding, Utah?
faq.answer: From Blanding, drive north on US 191 for approximately 12 miles and watch for BLM pullout access near mile marker 240 on the east side of the highway. Park at the informal pullout and hike east into the canyon wash. GPS coordinates (38.292112, -109.780914) and local online maps can help pinpoint the exact location if signage is unclear.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at Indian Canyon?
faq.answer: Bring a small hammer or crack chisel for carefully extracting specimens, a collection bag, and sturdy hiking boots with good grip for rocky terrain. A backpack is helpful for carrying finds. Most importantly, bring at least 2-3 liters of water per person, sunscreen, and a hat - the site offers no shade and sits in exposed high desert. A hand lens helps examine agate banding patterns.

meta_title: Indian Canyon Rockhounding - Utah Agate & Jasper
meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Blanding, Utah. Find agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens. No permit required, 25 lb daily limit. Family-friendly.
